author: @waterfuel
text: You should try building a replica of 1800's John Keely's self running hydraulic motor with useful output balanced flywheel. It had central rotating 4 way valve that produced 50lbs water hammer water pressure. Unit was seen running sawing wood, after alignment timing. patent application readable and can see sample model on You tube video. Also in new York years ago , there was a self running air compressor mounted on tank. It initially was hand turned until gauge had enough pressure in tank.40lbs output air ran air cylinder attached to crank on main compressor shaft keeping it going .The  balanced spoke type heavy rim flywheel had extra energy coming from machined 3 parts vortex tube that heated air chamber insulated for higher pressure output. Tubes are purchasable or read back issue science mechanics magazine article on home made adjustable units with various washer sizes for hot and cold.

author: @Greebstreebling
text: for such a huge wheel and all the energy you put into turning it, the power output (Volts x Amps) is tiny. The one radial coil you have running around the perimeter does ot cut the lines of flux produced by the fixed magnets. So if you arrange lots of coils across the periphery and more magnets, you'd really crack some power out. Also of course the friction in the system causes the wheel to slow down dramatically, so a levitation 'frictionless' bearing (with magnets) would increase the efficiency enormously.

author: @jonjudice1155
text: What you need to do is arrange the magnets on the spinning wheel so that north and south poles are on the left and right side then in a horse shoe arrange two coils one on each side. So what I'm saying is. An iron rod bent into a U shape and one coil on one side and one on the other then spin the wheel with the magnet passing in-between the coils. So that way you are utilizing both sides of the magnet. Then watch your results. Oh and the more coils the better. The more load you put on it the faster the wheel will spin and the amps will go down.

author: @andyhowlett2231
text: Rob - I'm confused. 4 mA at 11 volts is 44 mW of power, which is tiny considering the amount of work you put into spinning the flywheel. Do the turns of wire just run around the circumference of the wheel? If they do, no wonder the output is so low - the magnetic flux is meant to cut *across* the wire, not just run parallel to it. So you really need lots of small coils around the edge, not one big coil around it. Then you would get some real power out.

author: @franzschmidt6199
text: You could change from a bridge rectifier to a single diode and an electrolytic capacitor if you would arrange all of your magnets in the same direction (like North only or South only). By doing so you would automatically generate DC only and the diode would only have the purpose of preventing the capacitor from discharging back into the generating coil when the wheel stands still. Thank you for doing these experiments for us. You are an amazing teacher.

replies.author: @buffplums
replies.text: You can’t generate DC a from induction, you need to have a change in magnetic field otherwise there comes a point that the coils become saturated with the magnetic field and the only way to continue to get an output is to change the direction of the magnetic field and therefore the polarity of the current / voltage output of the coil.

replies.author: @captainTubes
replies.text: Afiak the "flywheel" Leeskhalin had was just a switching carousel. He only used that particular assembly for mining the blocks not lifting the blocks. But what it did was it switched the voltage on and off, bang bang,  to his large handmade induction coil solenoids that he used as power hammers and in that way he was able to mine the large blocks all by himself quite easily. With his power setup, I believe that he used electric winches along with block and tackle systems to move the large blocks beneath larger tripods. With these tools he employed tight strategy, and Godly supreme patience, to accomplish his goals, one, step, at, a, time.

replies.text: Yes that could work, do you mean? Or do you mean output? All things rely on the power the magnets produce ,which is unknown until measured, weight vs size and then magnetic strength, the more poles in series the more volt output in a single rpm. Hope that helps alittle.

replies.author: @tremail564
replies.text: The difference could be seen in the wave form on an oscilloscope  .as we come in contact with the north the wave rises to the peak around the middle of the north and then would fall if we kept moving  (placed a south next) IT would continue to fall until it bottoms out in the center of the south and as we keep moving IT would begin to rise again to the middle where we begun ( if another south is place and we keep moving) IT would start to fall to the trough(bottom of the wave value) and start back to middle. The distance between the poles would be how slowly IT waves and if too far IT may be a pulse   wave form.

author: @johndough8115
text:
To those that suggest adding a flywheel to a windmill... it would have to be done in a way that was disconnected from the Blades. IE:  If there is no wind, and you are trying to force the blades to spin anyways... then you are wasting energy by moving the resisting air... (rather than the air moving the blades)

 The place where you might add the flywheel... is in such a way that it still spins freely ONLY for the generator end of things... but does not actually spin the windmills blades.  That would allow momentum captured, to keep the power generation going... yet not be slowed by trying to move the wind-less Blades.

 The question is then... does it even matter?   If energy is stored into a typical battery cell... then a stop in the power flow, is not going to really effect anything.   As such, the heavy flywheel would be more of a system drag, than an enhancement.  Im no flywheel expert, so maybe Im not seeing the other potential benefits of it.

author: @robtitheridge9708

replies.author: @tremail564
replies.text: It has to do with it being in motion, and in order for energy to be made it has to change frequency (move) .the start of the coil has an input voltage in it and then as it spins it flow around the coil until it exits. 1+1= 0 ,0+0=0 / 1+0=1 ,0+1=1

text:
Omg! Imagine how much can it generate with the whole rim full of magnets... 👏

I've always thought it would be interesting to incorporate an electrostatic generator to power electromagnets in the rotor, in order to increase the magnetic field, and generate more power. The friction of the brushes would be minimal, though the increased complexity.

Very interesting those variations with the magnets arrangement, I wonder if using electromagnets and "shifting" the arrangement with a circuit could be of any use 🤔

replies.author: @kilokilos
replies.text: Yes an electrostatic generator for sure, some PVC pipes and a cotton brush might give you a kick

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Don't forget that the magnets are on a flywheel spinning really fast. Electromagnets in their place would mean powering a turning wheel, hard to supply

author: @DavyOneness
text: I hope you are planning to spin this up with that wind turbine you just built

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Have you considered adding a flywheel to your wind turbine to add constancy to the generation in periods of shifting wind speeds?

Lower positioning is going to lend less wind to the turbine, but so long as there is enough torque to turn the flywheel then the generation is going to be steady even if the wind speed drops. Just a thought mate, and combining both of your projects could lead to interesting results.

Well done mate!

replies.author: @gregtoja
replies.text: I was just about to write pretty much same comment :) Flywheel is perfect for wind turbines

author: @TrentTationnaiseXization
text: Thats a solid 5v supply. It's cool that the flywheel stores energy and smooths the signal.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
The flywheel certainly stores energy,  but the signal is AC, or recitified AC. and certainly not providr a smooth voltage signal.    

It takes other types of energy storage to smooth out say recitified AC to DC. My favorite is the pi filter.  the pi filter uses a capacitor to charge the voltage up quickly, followed by an inductor that opposes changes in current, and followed by yet one more capacitor to smooth out voltage changes across the inductor.  capacitors store energy in an electric field while inductors store energy in a magnetic field.

author: @conductiveinkalternative918
text: Could it be the wires in the coil are crossing each other giving the induction needed to generate voltage. It looks like the current stays steady with increasing speed.

author: @glennwebster1675
text: We live vicariously through you. Great video

author: @JasonJoplin
text: Robert I love your video, however if I may suggest moving your magnets closer to the center of the shaft of your flywheel and your coils closer to the center shaft of the flywheel to utilize that leverage of the flywheel more efficiently, with the magnets and load on the edge of the flywheel it makes the flywheel less efficient, by moving the load closer to the shaft allows you to convert that leverage into torque and you'll get a lot more torque to put on your load, great work I love to see everything you do, you and I think a lot alike, I have a similar setup just like this

replies.author: @fusiondensity3287
replies.text: The outer diameter of the wheel has the fastest speed for the magnets to pass by the coils

replies.author: @JasonJoplin
replies.text: @fusiondensity3287  yes, a negligible sacrafice
